content.text: Model Version and SDK Radar Tracking

Foundation model providers release new model versions frequently — sometimes monthly — while simultaneously deprecating older aliases with little warning. An application that hard-codes a model alias such as 'gpt-4' may silently switch to a different underlying model when the provider re-points that alias, or may fail entirely when the version is retired. Enterprise systems need a systematic approach to tracking model versions and the SDK surface that wraps them.

This document describes how to map individual model versions and SDK releases onto the four radar rings, how to detect deprecation events automatically, and how to build a version-pinning discipline that provides both stability and a clear migration path.

attrs.alt: Model version lifecycle and corresponding radar ring placement over time

content.text: Why Model Version Tracking Is an Architecture Concern

Model versioning differs from library versioning in two important ways. First, providers may change model behaviour (system prompt adherence, tool-call format, context window) without changing the model name — an unversioned alias is effectively a mutable reference. Second, deprecated models are often removed without a major-version bump in the SDK, because the SDK itself is still at the same version.
Silent behaviour drift: OpenAI's 'gpt-4' alias has pointed to different checkpoints over time. Regression tests that passed last quarter may silently fail on a new underlying model.
Alias retirement: 'gpt-3.5-turbo-0301' was removed; applications pinned to it received HTTP 404 errors at runtime.
Breaking output schema changes: tool-call JSON format, finish_reason values, and usage fields have changed across model versions — application code that parses these fields breaks silently.
SDK churn: Anthropic's Python SDK moved from the pre-1.0 API to 1.x with a complete method rename (completion → messages). Applications on the old SDK stopped receiving security patches.
Each major provider uses a different versioning scheme. Understanding the scheme is prerequisite to writing reliable pinning and monitoring code.
Provider can re-point to any checkpoint; do NOT pin for production
Immutable checkpoint; safe to pin; retired on published schedule

OpenAI publishes a model deprecation page with announced retirement dates. The recommended practice is to pin a date-versioned checkpoint in all environment configs and migrate to a newer checkpoint at least 30 days before the announced date.
Points to current latest within a tier; mutable
Mutable; rolls forward on Google's schedule
Experimental; no SLA; Assess ring only

Model versions fit the radar ring semantics differently from libraries. The primary signals are: production SLA availability, output API stability, enterprise compliance certification, and proximity to announced deprecation date.
Stable versioned ID; SOC 2 / HIPAA available; >12 months from announced EoL; output schema unchanged
Stable versioned ID but <12 months since GA; or compliance certification pending; pilot data shows acceptable quality
Preview / experimental suffix; no announced SLA; output schema may change
Announced EoL <90 days away; or already retired; or superseded by cost-equivalent Adopt-ring alternative

SDK releases carry their own risk profile independent of the underlying model. A major SDK version bump frequently signals breaking changes to method signatures, authentication, or response object shape.

# requirements/base.txt — pin minor, allow patch updates
openai>=1.40,<2.0
anthropic>=0.40,<1.0
google-generativeai>=0.8,<0.9
langchain>=0.2,<0.3
llama-index>=0.10,<0.11

# requirements/dev.txt — exact pins for reproducible CI
openai==1.51.0
anthropic==0.40.0
google-generativeai==0.8.3

Never pin to an exact version in requirements/base.txt that is shared across environments — you lose automatic security patches. Use minor-version ranges in base and exact pins only in the lock file (pip-compile output or poetry.lock).

Manual tracking of model retirement dates across providers does not scale. A lightweight checker can probe each provider's models endpoint on a schedule and alert when a pinned model transitions to deprecated or is removed.
Radar Check in CI: GitHub Actions
Running the model version check on a weekly schedule ensures deprecation events are caught before they impact production deployments.

# .github/workflows/model-radar-check.yml
name: Model Version Radar Check

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 9 * * 1"   # every Monday 09:00 UTC
  workflow_dispatch:        # allow manual trigger

jobs:
  check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Install deps
        run: pip install httpx

      - name: Run model radar check
        env:
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
        run: python scripts/model_radar_check.py

      - name: Notify Slack on failure
        if: failure()
        uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1
        with:
          payload: |
            {"text": "Model Radar Alert: a pinned model is approaching EOL. Check the workflow run for details."}
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL: ${{ secrets.SLACK_RADAR_WEBHOOK }}
Radar Entry Schema for Model Versions

Storing radar entries in a structured YAML file alongside your codebase makes the tech radar a code-reviewable artefact. The following schema captures enough information to automate ring computation and deprecation alerts.

# tech-radar/models.yaml
entries:
  - id: gpt-4o-2024-11-20
    provider: openai
    alias: gpt-4o
    ring: Adopt
    quadrant: Platforms & Cloud
    description: >
      Immutable checkpoint of GPT-4o released 2024-11-20.
      Supports structured output, vision, and tool use.
    versioned: true
    eol_date: null          # not yet announced
    sdk_min: openai>=1.40
    last_reviewed: 2025-04-01
    reviewed_by: platform-team

  - id: claude-sonnet-4-6
    provider: anthropic
    alias: claude-sonnet-4-6
    ring: Adopt
    quadrant: Platforms & Cloud
    description: >
      Current Sonnet tier; 200k-token context; strong instruction-following.
    versioned: true
    eol_date: null
    sdk_min: anthropic>=0.40
    last_reviewed: 2025-04-01
    reviewed_by: platform-team

  - id: gemini-2.0-flash-exp
    provider: google
    alias: gemini-2.0-flash-exp
    ring: Assess
    quadrant: Platforms & Cloud
    description: >
      Experimental preview; no SLA; output schema may change. Do not
      route production traffic until promoted to stable.
    versioned: false
    eol_date: null
    sdk_min: google-generativeai>=0.8
    last_reviewed: 2025-04-01
    reviewed_by: platform-team

  - id: gpt-3.5-turbo-0301
    provider: openai
    alias: gpt-3.5-turbo-0301
    ring: Hold
    quadrant: Platforms & Cloud
    description: >
      Retired 2024-06-13. Returns 404. Migrate to gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18.
    versioned: true
    eol_date: "2024-06-13"
    migration_target: gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18
    last_reviewed: 2025-04-01
    reviewed_by: platform-team
When a model moves from Adopt to Hold, a structured migration reduces risk. The typical window between deprecation announcement and removal is 90–180 days.
Update tech-radar/models.yaml: set ring to Hold, record eol_date and migration_target.
Identify all references: grep the codebase for the deprecated model ID string. Flag any hard-coded references in app code vs. environment configs.
Run regression suite against migration target: use the existing eval suite (if none exists, create a 50-item golden set from recent production logs).
Measure cost and latency delta: migration targets are often cheaper (gpt-4-turbo → gpt-4o-mini) or introduce latency changes. Document the delta.
Feature-flag the migration: deploy the new model ID behind a flag that defaults to the old ID. Enable for 5% → 25% → 100% traffic over two weeks.
Remove the flag and the old model ID string. Update radar entry ring to Adopt for the migration target.

import os

# Config-driven model selection — never hard-code model IDs in business logic
class ModelConfig:
    # Environment variable overrides allow migration via deploy config, not code changes
    CHAT_MODEL: str = os.getenv("LLM_CHAT_MODEL", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
    FAST_MODEL: str = os.getenv("LLM_FAST_MODEL", "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
    VISION_MODEL: str = os.getenv("LLM_VISION_MODEL", "gpt-4o-2024-11-20")

    # Radar ring declared alongside the default — visible to code reviewers
    # Adopt: claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, gpt-4o-2024-11-20
    # Trial: gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18
    # Do not add Assess or Hold models to defaults
Always pin date-versioned model identifiers (e.g. gpt-4o-2024-11-20) rather than mutable aliases (gpt-4o) in production configuration.
Map model versions to radar rings using four criteria: versioned ID, SLA/compliance, distance to EOL, and output schema stability.
Track SDK versions with minor-version range pins in shared requirements and exact lock files in CI.
Automate deprecation detection with a weekly script that probes provider model lists and compares against a local EOL registry; fail CI to trigger alerts.
Store radar entries in a YAML file under source control so ring changes are code-reviewed and auditable.
Use a feature-flag-based migration playbook: regression test → latency/cost delta → gradual rollout → flag removal.
